content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 207 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

March 19, 1979

Graham Ritchie
615 So. Flower St., Suite 1700
Los Angeles, California 90017

Dear Mr. Ritchie:

This is in response to our telephone conversation of
February 26, 1979, in which you requested advice on behalf of
Samuel Siegel concerning the conflict of interest provisions
of the Political Reform Act. This advice is provided pursuant
to Government Code Section 8314(b)1/. The facts as I understand them are as follows:

Mr. Siegel is a private attorney who represents the City
of Pico Rivera. The city pays him a retainer for which he
attends meetings of the City Council, reviews proposed ordinances and provides other routine legal advice in much the same
manner as an employed city attorney would. For work outside
the scope of the retainer, such as representing the city in
litigation, Mr. Siegel and the City Council separately negotiate
his fee on an hourly basis.

The City Council also serves as the Redevelopment Agency
for the City of Pico Rivera and Mr. Siegel also represents the
Redevelopment Agency on an hourly fee basis. The land within
the redevelopment area is currently zoned for agricultural
use and the redevelopment plan calls for the City Council to
rezone it for single family residences. Once the rezoning is
accomplished, the Redevelopment Agency will attempt to issue
bonds to finance the proposed development. You have indicated
that Mr. Siegel would like to propose to the Redevelopment
Agency that he handle the necessary legal work for the bond
issue under an agreement which would set his fee at a percentage of the issue. Such fee agreements are customary for bond

1/ All citations are to the Government Code unless otherwise
specified.

Graham Ritchie
March 19, 1979
Page Two


Counsel. Assuming that Mr. Siegel and the Redevelopment Agency
were to reach such an agreement, you have asked whether Mr. Siegel
would thereafter be allowed to advise the City Council with
respect to the rezoning which would have to take place before
redevelopment could begin.

Section 87100 of the Political Reform Act provides that:

    No public official at any level of state or local
    government shall make, participate in making or in
    any way attempt to use his official position to
    influence a governmental decision in which he knows
    or has reason to know he has a financial interest.

Section 82043 provides that "'Public official' means every
member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
government agency." By regulation, the Commission has defined
"consultant" to include "any natural person who provides, under
contract, information, advice, recommendation or counsel to a
state or local government agency..." 2 Cal. Adm. Code section
18700(a)(2).

Section 87103 provides in part that:

    An official has a financial interest in a decision
    within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
    reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
    have a material financial effect, distinguishable
    from its effect on the public generally, on:
    ...
    (c) Any source of income, other than loans by
    a commercial lending institution in the regular
    course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty
    dollars ($250) or more in value received by or
    promised to the public official within twelve months
    prior to the time when the decision is made;...

By regulation, the Commission has determined that a decision
will have a material financial effect on an official's source of
income if it will "directly affect the amount of income to be
received by the official...." 2 Cal. Adm. Code section 18700(a)
(3)(A). However, the term "income" is specifically defined in
the Act to exclude "[s]alary and reimbursement for expenses or
per diem received from i....local...government agency." Section
82030(b)(2). The question therefore becomes whether a fee paid

Graham Ritchie
March 16, 1979
Page Three


by a city and consisting of a negotiated percentage of a bond
issue should be considered to be salary received from the city.

Webster's New World Dictionary (2d Ed., 1974) defines
"salary" as "a fixed payment at regular intervals for services...,"
and Funk & Wagnalls Standard College Dictionary (1974) defines
it as "A periodic, fixed payment for services..." In discus-
sions of synonyms, both dictionaries agree that "wages" are
money paid to employees at short intervals (e.g. weekly, usually
for manual or unskilled labor; "salary" implies periodic pay-
ments to clerical and professional employees at longer inter-
vals; and "fee" applies to a payment for professional services
to someone who is self-employed, such as a doctor or lawyer.
One would not normally, therefore, use the word "salary" to
apply to the kinds of payments at issue here.

The purpose behind the exclusion from "income" of salary
from government agencies argues a different result, however.
If governmental salary were "income" under the Act, the agency
for which an official worked would become a "source of income"
under Section 87103, and the official would be disqualified
from participating in any decision which would have a material
financial effect on the agency. Clearly the policy of the Act
is to avoid such a result with respect to all government
employees, not just those who are clerical or professional
and to whose public earnings the word "salary" would normally
be applied.

On the other hand, the policies of the Act would not be
furthered by exempting all "income" received from government
agencies. For example, if an official were to supply low-
interest redevelopment loan administered by his agency, he
should be disqualified from any decision concerning the loan.
The exclusion of government salary from "income" cannot be
extended, therefore, beyond compensation for services rendered.
Payments to a retained City Attorney on an hourly basis, i.e.
tied to the amount of work performed for the city, are clearly
analogous to the salary received by public employees. A
percentage fee, not being tied to the amount of work performed,
is somewhat less like a salary, but it is a customary way to
compensate lawyers for their services in connection with a
bond issue. In addition, it is up to the agency involved to
determine the method by which they want to compensate those
who work for them, and they may believe that the chances are
such that the bond work will prove so time consuming that the
percentage fee is more favorable to the agency than an hourly
fee would be. That is a decision for the agency to make in
its fee negotiations, not one for the Commission to make in
application of the Political Reform Act.

Graham Ritchie
March 19, 1979
Page Four


     In short, the fees paid to Mr. Siegel for his services
as a lawyer for the City Council and for the Redevelopment
Agency, whether determined on an hourly or a percentage basis,
are "salary" and are therefore excluded from the term "income"
as used throughout the Political Reform Act. If you have any
further questions concerning the conflict of interest provi-
sions of the Political Reform Act, please do not hesitate to
call me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Sarah T. Cameron
                                        Counsel
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division

STC:mfa

sections.question: Whether Mr. Siegel would be allowed to advise the City Council with respect to rezoning if he had an agreement with the Redevelopment Agency to handle legal work for a bond issue under an agreement setting his fee at a percentage of the issue.
sections.conclusion: The fees paid to Mr. Siegel for his services as a lawyer for the City Council and for the Redevelopment Agency, whether determined on an hourly or a percentage basis, are 'salary' and are therefore excluded from the term 'income' as used throughout the Political Reform Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Can a private attorney who represents both a city council and its redevelopment agency advise on rezoning decisions if he has a contingent fee agreement (percentage of bond issue) with the redevelopment agency for future bond work?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC concludes that fees paid to a lawyer for services rendered to government agencies—whether hourly or percentage-based—constitute excluded 'salary' under the Political Reform Act, and therefore do not create a disqualifying financial interest in governmental decisions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Advice letter responding to telephone request; detailed legal analysis of whether percentage fees constitute 'income' or excluded 'salary' under Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103; uses dictionary definitions and statutory interpretation.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Mr. Siegel would be allowed to advise the City Council with respect to rezoning if he had an agreement with the Redevelopment Agency to handle legal work for a bond issue under an agreement setting his fee at a percentage of the issue.

CONCLUSION: The fees paid to Mr. Siegel for his services as a lawyer for the City Council and for the Redevelopment Agency, whether determined on an hourly or a percentage basis, are 'salary' and are therefore excluded from the term 'income' as used throughout the Political Reform Act.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that a private attorney representing a city council and redevelopment agency may advise on rezoning decisions even with a contingent percentage-fee agreement for future bond work, because such professional fees are excluded 'salary' under the Political Reform Act and do not constitute 'income' creating a financial interest.
